items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core meaning is mostly conveyed, but two key creative properties from the reference are weakened. (1) Register: «терзают смутные сомнения» is mock-heroic/high-brow; “nagging feeling” is everyday and flattens the theatrical self-dramatization that makes the line funny. (2) Aspect/implication: «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» here means each has (i.e., has lost/has had) the corresponding item; the joke is the parallelism of two losses. “has” reads as current possession in English, which can mislead and dull the detective-like callback. A more elliptical “Shpak—tape recorder; the ambassador—medallion” or “Shpak’s missing tape recorder; the ambassador’s missing medallion” would better preserve the gag.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation successfully preserves the core comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness and the absurd insistence on “Tsar” as a social-introduction label. It reads like a tipsy, stuck-in-a-loop greeting, which matches the reference analysis. Minor nuance: the reference leans into self-introduction (“I’m the Tsar”), while the provided version addresses the other as “Tsar,” which slightly shifts the joke’s target (Bunsha presenting himself vs. greeting the Tsarina). Still, the repetition and awkward etiquette remain funny and functional.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and perfectly matches the intended authoritarian, suddenly power-drunk persona.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Very close to the reference and preserves the high-register, archaic melancholy that clashes comically with the Soviet domestic setting. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than “Leave me,” but the overall tone (“old woman,” “in sorrow”) keeps the elevated, theatrical feel and the insult remains intact.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is broadly understandable (he feels sealed in and blames demons), but the construction is off in two ways. (1) “Bricked up!” in English strongly suggests slang/sexual meaning for many viewers, creating an unintended comedic effect and risking confusion. (2) The Russian implies passive victimhood (“They walled me in”), with “demons” as the agents; the translation drops the agentive framing and reads like a fragmentary exclamation. The intended joke (mystical interpretation of a mundane mechanical trap) would land better with “They’ve walled me in… walled me in, demons!” or similar.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and functional. It keeps the religious framing and the causal claim that the cross ‘did’ something. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still clearly elevated and fits the Tsar’s worldview; the joke remains.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the basic command to dance, but loses the specific performative/MC-like phrasing that makes it feel like a Soviet emcee opening the dance floor (and thus heightens the anachronistic clash). “Everybody dance!” is serviceable but more generic and less characterful than an announcement-style line (“Everyone to the dance floor!” / “All of you—dance!”).

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Good creative equivalent: it captures the breezy self-satisfaction and opportunistic timing that makes Miloslavsky likable as an anti-hero. Slightly more polite/neutral than the punchy colloquial “good call,” but still idiomatic and comic in context.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Imported caviar—eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key satirical contrast is mostly preserved (luxury caviar vs. humble eggplant). However, the reference note is important: calling it “caviar” in English can confuse viewers because “eggplant caviar” is not a common concept; the joke is that it’s a cheap spread presented as exotic. The translation keeps “caviar” and thus risks losing the ‘cheap Soviet spread’ punchline. A better solution would be “overseas spread—eggplant!” or “imported spread—eggplant!”

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: This segment is translated well overall in the full subtitle context: the escalating list and the final “three” are preserved, including the comic pause/hesitation around the suede jacket. The prestige-signaling “domestic” is kept. No substantive loss of the gag’s mechanics.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Matches the reference closely and preserves the calm, rhythmic, resigned escalation. The repetition lands and keeps the darkly comic undertone about psychiatric ‘treatment.’

summary.interpretation: 2.36 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.47 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
